item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of figured the timer is because the foundation has some concern that even reading about the man might trigger the effect. I agree that it wasn't really necessary but I can still see why it was added.

this was a seriously heartbreaking read.

and while maybe not to THIS extent, stuff like this happens every day. I was abused for most of my childhood and nobody believed me, i was made to spend time alone with my abuser and even made to apologize to him for lying about him, and lived in fear that he would make good on his threats of harming my family. this is not an uncommon tale. this is not an unbelievable story, not even with the foundation. if they'd only dealt with 3 things like this in a catalogue of thousands it doesn't surprise me that they hadn't cooked up failsafes, I don't think the risk would be statistically significant enough for them.

+1 for a beautiful and chilling tribute to your friend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kiran: While you were in the vicinity, did you see anyone suspicious, anyone at all?

SCP-3017-1-13: We only saw the firefighters.

Kiran: Are you absolutely sure?

SCP-3017-1-13: Yes, I'm pretty damn sure. The house was nearly coming down when I got there, so if someone set the fire, they were probably long gone at that point.

Haha, reading this a second time really makes this quite obvious

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only thing I don't like is the pointless timer at the end.
Adds nothing, makes no sense, isn't tied to the story…
Is just a random gimmick that kills the punch by making you wonder if there is more, but nope.

Other than that, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oof, this was a sad one.

I also like seeing how the Foundation completely fell for 3017's cognitohazard, even taking those capable of seeing through it as a threat. Even titans like the Foundation aren't immune to royally screwing up.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Finally took the time to sit down and read this one. Got blown away by the story and thought put into this. Simply amazing.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoy this. It exemplifies the humanity of the Foundation, it solidifies the idea that they are capable of making a grievous error. +1

item.title: the ending actually made me cry
(no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to like this, I really do. But I can't, really. This story has been done before, and thus I was able to guess all the important bits of the SCP by the time I was halfway through the containment procedures.

If it were any lesser organization, I'd have no trouble accepting it, but I have a hard time believing the Foundation doesn't have a failsafe for this kind of shit by now. Like, some sort of panic button made known to Level-Twos that sends out a "Help, my superiors are under the influence of a mind-affecting SCP!" signal for RAISA (or anyone else) to investigate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, there is one thing I don't understand. Ethics Commitee has never seen or spoke to SCP-3017, so they couldn't be "infected" right? And yet neither they nor anybody else didn't gave a single fuck about Kiran's unproffesional behaviour?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is utterly terrifying, and all the more so for being something that actually happens to real people. Not being believed, being constantly seen as guilty, and being repeatedly tortured by a faces institution—these are all horrifying things. I saw the twist coming and that just made reading on all the worse. Furthermore, the characterisation is good and the format of the document works well. Really enjoyed reading this.

Also I made a couple of minor typo fixes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ And this is what happens when the time passes over 1000.
- Absolutely Freakin' Nothing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Might not be addiction. Kiran believes that the kid holds crucial information of other GoIs, and is a "potential goldmine". Any other personnel might do the same as well I suppose, unless they know that the kid is in fact innocent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dr. Kiran seems to have developed an addiction to SCP-3017's effect but I have no idea why or how.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with the others about Kiran's motivation for the fire. I don't understand it and it's not spelled out why he would do it in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a lot of ways to make a good SCP, from making something that seems sufficiently "wrong" to making something impressive and cool or horrifying. But I think one of the best ways is to write a good story, something that speaks to people on a personal level and you've clearly done that. +1

By the way, if you haven't heard of it there's a podcast called Serial that you might like, it's a nonfiction kind of thing, with the first season focusing on the trial concerning the murder of Hae Min Lee, and the second on the search for Bowe Bergdahl, a soldier who was held by the Taliban for five years before being returned to the US and subsequently charged with desertion. It's pretty interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ehhh… I feel like it goes on for too long after the twist becomes obvious. I was confused as to how the entire 05 staff became infected without ever having met him, -or- why regular compartmentalization and internal checks and balances didn't catch the Foundation's mistake much earlier. All it takes is one dude looking at the situation objectively for the whole conspiracy to collapse in on itself. I'm not saying it doesn't work, just that in order to make it work, the guy's anomaly had to be pretty dang powerful. Either super-long range, affecting everyone all the time whether they've met him or not, or else memetic and propagated by any mention of him, even on paper or electronic records.

I feel like the fire kinda came out of nowhere. Like, to the point where I'm not sure if it was actually a Deus Ex Machina or if there's some motivation for the Foundation to have caused it that I'm not paranoid enough to figure out. (Why would they torch all their bargaining chips?)

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Well, I finished in 17th place with +125. I'm happy with this.

In honor of placing 17th, and because 17 is my second favorite number, I hereby rename this article, my highest rated and most successful article to date, as SCP-3017.

Once again, extensive and emphatic thanks to everyone who has read and supported this article. It's been a blast to see this article do so well. I also want to reiterate my thanks to Metaphysician and AJMansfeild for helping with coding the ending.

Finally, I wish to thank Christopher Moshe Elliott, who not only helped inspire this article, but also helped teach me what's truly important in life. This was my first entry on the site since he passed away this past August.

Congratulations to the winners and the runners up. Thanks for the wonderful stories.

EDIT 3 years later I find typos, lol.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, whatever floats your boat. Thanks for reading! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did get that — I just thought the Foundation should have guessed it as fast as I did, since the trope has come up so much that it's hard to suspend my disbelief about the Foundation having not encountered this before and prepped better. Even if, like I said, that may be an unfair meta-assessment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be clear, Frasier was not an ordinary person. There was, in fact, an anomaly, even if it was only perceptual.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's nothing wrong with this article — but it didn't work for me because it felt excessively cruel and overly manipulative in a way that I didn't find effective. The problem with SCPs like this is that I feel this sort of thing happens to the Foundation in-universe so much that I feel them falling for it so easily (and being so absurdly cruel to go with it) is not particularly believable any longer. Of course, this is a meta concern — as far as the article is concerned, this could be much rarer.

The central character also feels very generic; that's an acceptable choice for letting people see themselves more easily in him (and therefore feel more horrified), but that also didn't work on me.

Well-crafted, however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have some misgivings about this, in particular I don't understand Kiran's motivation for the fire, and I feel like the fact that 3000 was innocent was telegraphed far too early. Otherwise this does enough right with the fear of injustice to earn an upvote.

item.title: Author Post: +100
item.content:encoded:
This article was, without a doubt, my favorite one to write. I am ecstatic to see it be my most well received work on the site thus far. To reach 100 in just over two weeks is mind-blowing to me.

Thanks again to everyone who read, rated, and commented. You all rock.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for pointing that out. I'll double check with the staff to see if I can edit that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rylan: Good afternoon, SCP-3000. How are you feeling?

SCP-3000: I fell like the time Beck caught me in the eye when we were playing catch.

I'm pretty sure this is supposed to say "feel"? Not sure what site policy is on editing active competition pages.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Being unjustly incarcerated and completely unable, no matter what, to reason one's way out of it despite being innocent, is something I personally find very frightening. Well done on the gradual ramping-up of nastiness, the sour conclusion and the simple ultimate reveal. 100% credible. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: http://i.imgur.com/nRp4l2m.jpg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The person who set the fire reminds me of how much I hated 1337 because of the extreme attempt at neutralization, except this is even crueler and also reads way more sadistic for the Foundation, regardless of your attempts to convey why it happened. Yeah, this article conveys horror really well, but that part killed all my interest in this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the evil Foudation!

I love the part the staff abusing this poor guy, forcing him to tell something he never know and he can not reject!

I aslo love the part that fire his whole family after his escape to destroy his last hope! That's the price for freedom, fighter!

THE most important part: tortrue the innocent without any payment or ethic problems, because we are SCP foudation! Fuck the justice! HAHAHAHAHA

nice work really.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It relates to the symptoms listed at the top of the page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ooh, I am loving this take on the horror theme. All other proposals I've read so far used the SCP itself as the source of horror. You know, big scary keters. This one uses the fear of Fraiser's fate possibly happening to anyone. I still prefer other proposals for the 3000 spot but this one also gets +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there any point to the time counter on the bottom of the page besides "this article might be a cog hazard in of itself?"

That seems particularly strange, particularly since no other SCPs have it and this SCP is neutralized.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hun you're making me feel guilty. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: #JusticeForFrasier

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, right. Kiran did it.

I missed it at first because I don't understand his motivation for burning down 3000's previous residence. As far as I can see, the compulsion by 3000-A is to incarcerate 3000, not to bring harm to it or 3000-1. Harming 3000-1 or destroying the residence would only make it harder to recapture it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awesome it's posted \o/ , as told you I liked it, so here you got +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I spotted that immediately, checked to make sure it fit, and came straight here to see if it had been mentioned.

Apparently not.

Jeeez. There's one individual in this narrative that needs a kicking… Maybe Clef will have a word with them when he hears of this Scip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't want to give it away, but if you read the previous addendum carefully, I've hinted as to who started the fire.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Solid stuff. +1

The one critique I'd have is that the arson seemed incongruent with the rest of the narrative. (Unless I'm missing something.) Was it just a coincidental fire? Who started it?

I would have preferred a slightly different ending, where the SCP killed himself post-escape to specifically avoid having his loved ones harmed in the course of his recapture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man this is good. I liked the touch near the end where Kiran kinda regretted what happened. I was entirely ready to write her off as the whole 'Foundation worker is a sadist who uses her job as an excuse' thing, but the final interview put it into perspective. Fuckin excellent article man, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: THIS. Good god. I think anyone who doesn't think this is horror hasn't been told that their perception of reality is wrong when you know it's not. Hell, it's a major piece of the horror genre—we all know the house is haunted, but the police won't believe you if you tell them that. The idea that the Foundation, in all its credulity toward portals to flesh-temples and parasitic youtube videos and crumbling cities only accessible in dreams and virulently contagious religions and an unkillable adapting lizard, would disbelieve this man? That's horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now this is an ethics dilemma that I can get behind. All other writers do this pathetic game where they pitch some kind of ritual to contain a certain anomaly and how sacrificing D-Class is supposed to satisfy it while also nudging at us to feel bad because of how brutal the idea is. This is a lot more realistic and it's got a lot of room for discussion.

I especially like the fact that Kiran took interrogation to such an extreme, and I think the idea that it was all a misunderstanding could easily be traced to the doctor's shitty questioning tactics. It was easy to see that the guy just didn't have the info and that he might not have even been anomalous, but I like to interpret this as maybe what happens when Foundation types decide they want to get their hands a little too dirty.

+1, replace other writers pls

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jesus Christ how horrifying.

The Foundation as a bad guy is not necessarily a new idea, but how this articles goes about it really takes that premise to a chilling place. The worst part, in my opinion, is that its not the Foundation's fault that they became villains here and it was only after they done goofed pretty royally that they realized the real impact of that they have done, to what amounted to little more than a normal dude.

+1. Good show.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I absolutely loved this, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's mostly just a bookend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this quite a bit. I actually had an idea with a very similar theme that never got off the ground.

If anything, I'd shorten it up a bit. I realized the "twist" like 1/3 of the way through.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh oh oh. This started off at horror and just kept going.

The final twist fulfilled the promise of the premise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, the horror that hit me was that of no one believing you. The effect of this kid was so strong that none of the researchers were willing to accept that he didn't have the information they were seeking. The whole breakdown of the system was great, and this line

At the recommendation of Dr. Kiran, Researcher Rylan has been redesignated SCP-3000-1-13 and placed under containment.

really sells it. Upvoted for that and the final bit with the counter, though there are a number of wording and phrasing issues that could stand to be looked at.

item.title: I like it but...
item.content:encoded: As I said in chat, I really like this SCP, but I don't think it really fits the horror theme. If it was a regular SCP I'd up-vote, but as its submitted in the contest I wont at this time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this weaves together three types of horror quite deftly:

  • Kafkaesque existential horror (the -A effect)
  • sentimental horror (loved ones in peril)
  • the dread of realizing that a basically omnipotent paramilitary organization dedicated to preserving baseline reality might get things horribly wrong, and who can say it won't happen again?

Excellent work. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there some gimmick to the timer at the bottom that I'm overlooking due to impatience and/or being on mobile?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not really horror in the traditional sense, but I'd say it works pretty well (admittedly, I'm kinda biased since this involves my biggest fear, but still). +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a sucker for the Foundation succumbing to an SCP, however temporarily
+1

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
If this doesn't win, I'd very much like the SCP-3333 slot. (Yes, I'm aware I cannot call any specific slot yet.)

Thanks to Metaphysician for helping me with brainstorming this idea, and to AJMansfield for helping me with some coding. Also thanks to my dad for the suggestion I torture this poor man's cat. I didn't end up doing that, but still, thanks I guess.

Image Credits
Hide

First image is the mugshot of a convicted bank robber from Kentucky, public domain.

Second image courtesy of user albertstraub on Flickr, and licensed under Creative Commons. https://www.flickr.com/photos/11419501@N02/16873761888

Some of my thought process when writing this (SPOILERS)
Hide

It is often said that when writing horror, one should write about what personally scares them. Besides spiders, what scares me is the thought of losing a loved one. Since this past August, it's been a fear I cannot shake, and I've attempted to convey that here.

For better or for worse. In order for such a personal horror to be effectively presented, the subject must be sympathetic and well characterized. Frasier Melbrook represents is the most work I've ever attempted to give real personality to a character. My hope is that the extended addenda have effectively conveyed this; whether or not I did a good job is up to you.

There was a second, more subtle avenue explored here, which was the idea of the Foundation itself becoming compromised. If the characterization fell flat for you, I hope this element was enough to carry the article.

I know that some people will read this as more tragedy than horror. To me, those two concepts are very much intertwined. Horror is the fear of tragedy, in one form or another, and I confess I may not have effectively separated them. I can only hope that, if this isn't good horror, it's at least good tragedy. But that is up to you.
